title: 27 Meskerem — The Monthly Crucifixion & The Assembly

title": "27 Meskerem — The Monthly Crucifixion & The Assembly

narrative": "title\": \"27 Meskerem — The Monthly Crucifixion & The Assembly

narrative\": \"Meskerem

The Monthly Commemoration of the Holy Crucifixion

St. Callistratus and the Forty-Nine Martyrs

St. Epicharis the Martyr

On the twenty-seventh of Meskerem, the Church stands again before the Cross in the monthly Crucifixion commemoration — and in the month of Maskal, this monthly remembrance carries a unique quality. The Maskal Cross blazed on the hilltops ten days ago in royal triumph; today the same Cross is contemplated in its original and irreducible reality: the wood on which the Son of God died. The Demera fire is still warm in the memory; the Maskal processions are still vivid in the imagination; and now the Church strips away the celebration and stands before what is beneath all celebration — the bare wood, the nails, the cry of dereliction, the darkness at midday, the curtain torn, the blood and water. The monthly Crucifixion is the calendar's insistence that no feast, however glorious, can be permitted to replace the event it celebrates. Maskal is real, and its triumph is real; but it is the triumph of this, and the memory of this must not be softened by the triumph's brightness.

St. Callistratus and the Forty-Nine Martyrs are one of the great corporate martyrdom narratives of the early Church. Callistratus was a soldier who was discovered praying by his fellow soldiers at night, was thrown into the sea in a sack, was miraculously preserved and emerged unharmed, and was then — in the tradition's account — joined in his confession by forty-nine of his fellow soldiers who had witnessed the miracle and converted on the spot. They were subsequently executed together. The corporate quality of this martyrdom — one man's witness producing forty-nine further witnesses, one preserved life generating forty-nine surrendered lives — is one of the most powerful demonstrations in the entire martyrology of the contagious quality of holy courage. The Cross is not a solo act; it generates a community of the Cross, a fellowship of those who are willing to go where it leads.

St. Epicharis was a woman of Rome who was tortured for information about a conspiracy against the Emperor Nero — a conspiracy she may not even have been directly part of — and who endured the torture in silence, not betraying a single name. She was brought back on the second day of interrogation in a chair, her body broken, and continued to refuse. She finally strangled herself in the chair with a cloth from her garment to prevent further attempts to break her silence. Her story is a pagan woman's martyrdom in the ethical rather than the explicitly theological sense, yet the Church has recognized in her silence a form of the same refusal that the martyrs displayed before their persecutors. On the monthly Crucifixion day, Epicharis's unbreakable silence is the pagan world's testimony that the values the Cross embodies — the refusal to betray the innocent, the acceptance of suffering rather than complicity — are not exclusively Christian but are recognized, in moments of extremity, by every human soul that has kept its humanity intact.

The monthly Crucifixion, Callistratus and his forty-nine, and Epicharis together on the twenty-seventh of Meskerem present the Cross in its full complexity: the cosmic event of redemption, the community it generates through witness, and the human truth it recognizes even beyond its own boundaries. The golden light of Meskerem has been real and beautiful, and the Cross that shines within it is real and beautiful, and it is also the wood on which a man died in agony. The community that holds all of this together — triumph and cost, fire and blood, the golden daisy and the crown of thorns — is the community that has understood what the month of Meskerem is actually teaching.
